content.text:
Assuming that we keep the version 2b, what is the next experiment we should run according to the plan?  (new branch, please)
I wonder, what if we could add block markers since we were able to, uh, draw consistent guidelines? Maybe we can also add the markers. It would be nice to have separate marker symbols for headings, paragraphs, call out tables, and so on so that they're... they don't have to be the same as the lists, but they could, umstill add some visual queue for the user.
I'll look for the experiment plan document to see what's next after 2b.

input.command: git log --oneline -20 && echo "---" && find . -iname "*plan*" -not -path "*/node_modules/*" -not -path "*/.git/*" 2>/dev/null

result.output:
1	# Decoration experiments: plan (2026-07-13)
2	
3	Answers the postmortem's open question — is cross-kind visual unification achievable, and
4	how — by running small, isolated, falsifiable prototypes instead of another multi-attempt
5	scramble. See [06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md](06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md)
6	for what failed and why, and the git history of this doc's own introduction for the
7	external research that shaped this plan (obsidian-outliner's measure-don't-fight technique,
8	Logseq's per-block-component architecture, Silverbullet's declined-to-unify precedent).
9	
10	## Ground rules for every experiment below
11	
12	1. **One technique per experiment, isolated.** Each gets its own branch off a clean
13	   baseline (outline mode + grammar + structural ops, zero decoration code) so results are
14	   attributable and comparable, not entangled the way attempt 1→2→3 was last time.
15	2. **A fixed, shared fixture corpus, screenshotted every time — not just the fixture for
16	   the thing being fixed.** Every experiment runs against the *same* corpus (below), in
17	   both bundled light and dark themes. Growing the corpus is fine; shrinking scope of what
18	   gets screenshotted is not.
19	3. **A real-vault pass is mandatory before an experiment is called done**, not just the
20	   synthetic corpus. This was the single biggest false-confidence source last time.
21	4. **Record results in the comparison table at the bottom before moving to the next
22	   experiment.** Verdict is one of: keep, reject, or needs-follow-up — with the concrete
23	   reason.
24	5. **No experiment is "done" on green tests alone.** Unit tests here can only prove the
25	   pure computation is internally consistent; they cannot prove anything about rendering.
26	   Screenshot + real-vault verification is the actual gate.
27	
28	## The fixture corpus (shared across all experiments)
29	
30	- **Flat**: 3 top-level paragraphs, no lists or headings at all — the original motivating
31	  bug (`04-open-questions.md`: "with no bullets/indent chrome, outline mode is hard to
32	  verify by eye in flat documents").
33	- **Mixed**: heading → heading → nested bullet list → paragraph-as-sibling-of-list-item →
34	  code fence, matching `MIXED_MD` in the existing e2e spec.
35	- **Checkbox/task**: a top-level paragraph followed by a checkbox list, mixed
36	  checked/unchecked — the exact shape that broke in the field last time.
37	- **Heading-then-list, no paragraph**: a list directly under a heading with *no*
38	  intervening paragraph — isolates the "list item's native indentation doesn't know about
39	  heading ancestors" case, the crux of the additive-margin hypothesis.
40	- **Multi-line continuation**: a paragraph and a list item each spanning 2+ lines via
41	  `Shift+Enter` — never actually screenshotted last time; explicitly in scope now.
42	- **Wide numbering**: an ordered list crossing a digit-width boundary (items 9 and 10) —
43	  checks whether any supplemental indentation clashes with native marker-width variance.
44	- **Deep nesting**: 4+ levels mixing bullet and ordered markers.
45	
46	Committed once as [e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts](../../e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts) —
47	exported markdown-string constants (matching the project's existing `createNote(path,
48	content)` convention, not on-disk `.md` files) plus an `ALL_DECORATION_FIXTURES` array for
49	"screenshot everything" loops. Every experiment branch imports from here; don't fork or
50	re-type fixtures per experiment, or results stop being comparable.
51	
52	---
53	
54	## Experiment 1 (primary): additive-only indentation, no synthetic marker
55	
56	**Hypothesis**: your proposed compromise — drop the marker for non-list kinds entirely,
57	keep list items' native marker completely untouched, and get indentation right by *adding*
58	to what's there rather than *replacing* it.
59	
60	**Mechanism**:
61	- Paragraphs/headings/atoms: `padding-left` (atoms: `margin-left`) = `depth × unit`. Purely
62	  additive — nothing native to fight here, this part already worked in the D9 walk-back.
63	- List items: **never touch `text-indent`/`padding-left`.** Compute a new quantity —
64	  *supplemental depth* = total tree depth − depth-within-nearest-list-root (i.e. how many
65	  non-list-item ancestors sit above the nearest list root) — and apply `margin-left` =
66	  `supplemental-depth × unit` on top of native rendering. A list with no heading/paragraph
67	  ancestors above its root gets supplemental depth 0 → byte-identical to
68	  outline-mode-off, which becomes a permanent regression invariant, not a one-time check.
69	
70	**New code needed**: `decorate.ts`'s walk already computes total depth; add a second
71	counter that resets to 0 whenever a list-item ancestor chain starts, to get
72	depth-within-nearest-list-root. Keep the existing pure module's shape (no CM6 imports);
73	this is a pure-function change, unit-testable the same way the current tests are.
74	
75	**Success criteria**:
76	- Every fixture's non-list lines render additively (no property replaced, only added).
77	- Heading-then-list fixture: the list visibly shifts right by the heading's depth
78	  contribution, while its own internal per-level spacing is pixel-identical to
79	  outline-mode-off.
80	- Wide-numbering fixture: no overlap/misalignment between marker and text.
81	- Multi-line continuation: continuation lines indent identically to their node's first
82	  line, in both directions (paragraph and list item).
83	
84	**What would kill it**: if `margin-left` on a list item interacts badly with anything
85	Obsidian's own list CSS assumes about the box it's positioned in (e.g. the fold-indicator's
86	absolute positioning might be relative to a box `margin-left` moves but `padding-left`
87	wouldn't have) — check the fold-indicator specifically, since it's the one native element
88	that already burned an attempt this cycle.
89	
90	## Experiment 2 (primary): guide lines — two implementations, head to head
91	
92	Build **both** below against the same corpus and compare; don't pick a favorite in advance.
93	
94	**2a — overlay-measured (obsidian-outliner's proven technique).** A `ViewPlugin` reads
95	already-rendered pixel positions via `view.coordsAtPos()`/`view.lineBlockAt()` *after*
96	Experiment 1's indentation has been laid out, and draws absolutely-positioned overlay
97	`<div>`s (inline-styled from JS, not CSS classes) in a layer outside `.cm-content`.
98	Recompute debounced on `docChanged || viewportChanged`, not every `ViewUpdate` (a real
99	perf/correctness improvement over this cycle's D4 choice, now that the mode-toggle-poke
100	problem can be solved with a dedicated `StateEffect` instead of an empty dispatch forcing a
101	full recompute path every time).
102	
103	**2b — CSS stacked-gradient (the `@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers` technique).** A
104	single `Decoration.line` per line sets one CSS custom property to a comma-joined list of
105	`repeating-linear-gradient(...)` layers (one per ancestor level), consumed by a single
106	`::before` — O(1) DOM nodes per line regardless of depth, no JS pixel measurement at all.
107	
108	**Compare on**: correctness against the multi-line continuation fixture specifically (2a
109	gets wrapped-row height for free via `lineBlockAt`; 2b needs the line's own rendered height,
110	which should also be free since it's one element per whole line — verify this isn't
111	secretly harder); resilience across light/dark theme and a couple of community themes if
112	convenient; code size/complexity; whether 2b's reliance on the cascade re-triggers *any* of
113	the `!important`/specificity problems from last time (it shouldn't, since guides are a new
114	property no native rule contests — but verify, don't assume).
115	
116	**Success criteria**: guide lines visibly connect a node to its ancestors' guides at every
117	fixture's nesting, including the multi-line continuation case (never verified last time).
118	
119	## Experiment 3 (conditional — only run if Experiment 1's flat fixture fails review)
120	
121	**Trigger**: if dropping the marker means the **flat** fixture (3 top-level paragraphs, all
122	at depth 0, so indentation alone conveys nothing) still doesn't read as "outline mode is
123	on" — which is the *original* motivating bug this whole change exists to fix — try a
124	minimal, low-risk marker that avoids the em/rem bug class *by construction*: a fixed-px-
125	width left border or background tint (not a `::before` glyph with a font-size), so there's
126	no font-size context to inherit incorrectly regardless of which kind's line it's on.
127	
128	**Success criteria**: flat fixture reads as distinct nodes at a glance, in a screenshot,
129	without reintroducing any font-size-dependent sizing.
130	
131	**If this also isn't good enough**: fall back to the overlay-measured marker (same
132	mechanism as 2a, sized as a fixed absolute value, never inheriting font-size by
133	construction) — this is the "real" fix for the original marker-size bug, just held in
134	reserve since it's more implementation work than the compromise you proposed.
135	
136	## Experiment 4 (optional risk-spike — only if Experiment 1 still feels fragile on real content)
137	
138	Spike the `Decoration.widget` spacer technique (real inline content pushing text via layout
139	width, instead of any CSS box-model property) in isolation, specifically against the
140	multi-line/wrapped-continuation fixture, to test the one confirmed real gotcha found in
141	research: CM6 wraps every widget in an invisible `.cm-widgetBuffer` that can introduce
142	stray line-wrap points. Go in expecting this might kill the idea — it's unproven in the
143	wild for exactly this reason, and the whole CM6 community converged on CSS `Decoration.line`
144	instead. Only worth spending time on if Experiment 1's `padding-left`/`margin-left`
145	approach keeps showing cascade fragility against real themes despite the additive-only
146	discipline.
147	
148	## Track 5 (parallel infrastructure, not gated — apply to all of the above)
149	
150	- Commit the fixture corpus once; every experiment's verification re-screenshots all of it,
151	  not just its own new fixture.
152	- One automated check: render two elements of deliberately different font-size (a heading
153	  line and a paragraph line) at the same computed tree depth, diff *every*
154	  decoration-related computed style at once (`padding-left`, `margin-left`, any marker
155	  `font-size`/`width`/`height`). This single mechanism would have caught both the
156	  indentation em/rem bug and the marker-size em/rem bug last time, instead of two separate
157	  manual catches.
158	- Fix `npm run build` to actually invoke esbuild, or add an explicit pre-verification step
159	  (`npm run build:plugin && npm run vault:install`) to the checklist so a stale bundle
160	  can't produce a false "verified" result again.
161	- A real dev-vault pass is part of *every* experiment's exit criteria, not a final step
162	  after all experiments conclude.
163	
164	## Architecture question: per-block editor tree — resolved, not prototyped
165	
166	Researched via your `continuous-journal` plugin as the concrete test case (see chat).
167	Verdict: not a lighter alternative to the already-rejected custom-view path — it requires a
168	comparable-or-larger private-API footprint (leaf-grafting, `setActiveLeaf` monkey-patching)
169	plus a genuinely unsolved new problem (N editors bound to overlapping ranges of one shared
170	file, kept consistent as any one of them edits). Shelved against this project's decided
171	100%-public-API bar (Q1); revisit only if that bar itself is ever renegotiated.
172	
173	## Results
174	
175	Quick-scan status; full detail in the subsections below.
176	
177	| # | Technique | Status | Verdict |
178	|---|---|---|---|
179	| 1 | Additive indentation, no marker | Done, three real bugs found and fixed | **Keep** |
180	| 2a | Guides — overlay-measured | Done, three real bugs found and fixed | **Keep** |
181	| 2b | Guides — CSS stacked-gradient | Done, full corpus coverage confirmed (blockquote, community themes, gap continuity, and table all fixed + confirmed live) | **Keep** — full parity with 2a's coverage; simpler and smaller code, zero pixel measurement |
182	| 3 | Minimal marker fallback (conditional) | Not triggered | Deprioritized — see below |
183	| 4 | Widget-spacer spike (optional) | Not triggered | No fragility observed that would call for it |
184	
185	### Experiment 1 — additive indentation, no marker
186	
187	**Verdict: keep.** All 8 corpus fixtures pass, both themes, screenshotted and visually
188	reviewed (not just DOM-asserted) — see
189	[e2e/specs/50-decorations.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/50-decorations.e2e.ts). The user toured
190	every note in the real `test-vault` by hand — the actual dev-vault pass the postmortem
191	insisted on, not a formality — and found three real bugs the synthetic corpus missed
192	entirely (below). All three are fixed, with permanent fixtures/assertions added so they
193	can't silently regress. User's assessment after the fixes: "much more promising" than the
194	prior attempt — indentation alone gives real hierarchy legibility switching modes on and
195	off, more than initially expected going in.
196	
197	**Code cost**: ~15 lines added to `decorate.ts` (2 new fields + 1 extra walk parameter),
198	~150-line `decorations.ts` adapter (a `StateField` for plain lines plus a `ViewPlugin` for
199	widget-replaced atoms), ~25-line `styles.css`.
200	
201	**Bugs found and fixed** (chronological — each was caught only by a human looking at real,
202	organic content, never by the synthetic corpus or DOM assertions):
203	
204	1. **Cascade fight, caught in the synthetic corpus.** Obsidian's own `app.css` has
205	   `.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > * { margin: 0px !important; }` — three
206	   classes of specificity, which beat our original two-class `.cm-line.to-decor-*` selector
207	   outright even with `!important` on both sides. Fixed by matching Obsidian's own
208	   ancestor-chain specificity, not by escalating `!important` further (there's nowhere
209	   further to escalate — the tie-break past equal `!important` is specificity, then source
210	   order).
211	2. **Tables and callouts not indenting at all, caught by the user in the real vault.**
212	   Obsidian renders tables, callouts, raw HTML blocks, and (surprisingly) horizontal rules
213	   as opaque replacement widgets in Live Preview (`.cm-embed-block`, or `.hr` for the rule,
214	   which oddly still carries a `cm-line` class despite being widget-rendered). A
215	   `Decoration.line` targeting that source line has **no effect at all** — not even a
216	   partial class-merge win, confirmed live by dumping the DOM: both class and inline style
217	   come back completely empty. Code fences and plain blockquotes are genuinely plain
218	   `.cm-line`s and were unaffected, which is exactly why the synthetic corpus (which only
219	   exercised those two atom kinds) never caught this. Fixed with a companion `ViewPlugin`
220	   that finds these widgets directly after each render (`docViewUpdate`, not the pre-render
221	   `update()` hook — DOM reads/writes need the widget to already exist) and sets
222	   `margin-left` inline with `!important`, which always wins over *any* stylesheet rule
223	   regardless of specificity — a clean escape hatch for exactly this class of native-widget
224	   cascade fight. It reads the same `--to-decor-unit` CSS variable the class-based rules
225	   use, so the unit constant stays single-sourced.
226	3. **Table still visibly offset from code/callout after fix #2, caught by the user.** Once
227	   tables got a margin at all, they still sat slightly further right than a same-depth code
228	   block or callout. Root cause: `.cm-embed-block.cm-table-widget` carries its own native
229	   left padding (for the row/column drag-handle icons) that callout's wrapper doesn't have.
230	   Padding never moves an element's own box — invisible for code/callout, whose *background*
231	   fills their whole padded box — but the table's actual visible content (a `<table>` nested
232	   two levels inside the padded wrapper) sits in normal flow within that padding, so it
233	   visually renders offset by exactly that amount. Fixed by reading the widget's own
234	   `getComputedStyle(...).paddingLeft` live (not a hardcoded pixel constant, which would be
235	   theme/version-fragile) and subtracting it from the applied margin, clamped at zero so a
236	   depth-0 atom never goes negative.
237	
238	**Deferred, not fixed on this branch**: list items sit visually further right than a
239	sibling paragraph/blockquote at the same tree depth. Traced to a real file
240	(`Kitchen Renovation.md`) and confirmed via measurement that our own added margin is
241	*identical* across all three at that depth — the extra offset is Obsidian's own native
242	list-item hang (`text-indent: -Npx` / `padding-left: Npx`), which already pushes list text
243	right of a sibling paragraph's text in **vanilla Obsidian, mode on or off**. It's not
244	something this experiment introduced; it's just more noticeable now that everything else
245	lines up precisely. Fixing it the way `obsidian-outliner`'s `BetterListsStyles` does means
246	touching that native `text-indent`/`padding-left` pair directly — the single riskiest
247	surface the postmortem blamed for most of the prior failed attempt's bugs. Left alone
248	pending a deliberate, isolated follow-up; see
249	[open question](#open-question-shrinking-only-our-own-added-list-margin) below for a
250	narrower variant that stays additive-only.
251	
252	Also confirmed **not** a bug: the flat fixture (3 top-level paragraphs, all depth 0) shows
253	no visual change from outline-mode-off. This was flagged going in as Experiment 3's
254	trigger condition, but the user confirmed on real content that this reads correctly the
255	moment a heading sits above the paragraphs — depth-0-with-no-ancestor genuinely has nothing
256	to convey, which is correct, not a legibility gap.
257	
258	### Experiment 2a — guide lines via pixel-measured overlay
259	
260	**Verdict: keep.** Branched off Experiment 1 (`experiment/decorations-2a-guides-overlay`).
261	All 8 corpus fixtures plus 4 real vault notes pass, both themes, screenshotted and visually
262	reviewed at pixel level (not just DOM-asserted) — see
263	[e2e/specs/51-guides-overlay.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/51-guides-overlay.e2e.ts), 43/43 e2e
264	tests green across the full suite (not just this spec). One ancestor-with-children gets one
265	continuous overlay `<div>` spanning from its first child's own line to its deepest
266	descendant's last line, positioned by *measuring* (`view.coordsAtPos`/`view.lineBlockAt`)
267	the ancestor's own already-rendered position rather than recomputing depth × unit —
268	obsidian-outliner's technique, applied to this project's universal (not list-only) tree.
269	
270	**Real-vault finding, fixed on this branch (not a separate follow-up)**: a *list-item*
271	ancestor gets no guide of its own, deliberately. The user's own real-vault comparison
272	(built-in indent guides only / both / ours only, screenshotted side by side) showed two
273	problems, both traced to the same cause: (a) with Obsidian's native "Show indent guides"
274	setting on, list nesting got visibly doubled lines; (b) even alone, our guides within a
275	list read as unevenly spaced, because native list nesting has its own internal per-level
276	width that our fixed unit doesn't match (the same native-hang mismatch Experiment 1 already
277	deferred rather than fight, now visible one layer up). Native indent guides already connect
278	one bullet precisely to the next *within* a list — there is no gap for a second mechanism to
279	usefully fill there. Fixed by having `computeGuides` skip pushing a guide when the anchor
280	node's `kind === 'list-item'`, while still including list-item subtrees in a *non-list*
281	ancestor's own span (a heading or paragraph bridging into a list still gets its guide,
282	reaching all the way through — only the within-list levels get none of their own). This
283	turns, e.g., the `deep-nesting` fixture (four levels, all list items) into zero guides at
284	all — entirely deferred to native — while `heading-then-list` keeps exactly one guide
285	(the heading's, spanning the whole list) instead of three.
286	
287	**Deliberate deviation from the plan's literal wording**: the plan called for "a dedicated
288	`StateEffect`" for the out-of-band mode-toggle refresh. Dispatching a raw CM6 effect from
289	`main.ts` (application code, not a registered extension) has no path through Obsidian's
290	public `Editor` API — `EditorTransaction` exposes no `effects` field, and reaching into
291	`(editor as any).cm.dispatch()` is exactly the private-API surface this project's own
292	research already flags to avoid outside registered extensions
293	([03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md](03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md)). Used per-instance state
294	comparison instead (`ViewPlugin`/`StateField` each remember the outline-mode flag they last
295	saw and compare on every transaction — cheap, since it's just a boolean read, not a
296	reparse): functionally identical gate (`docChanged || viewportChanged || mode-just-toggled`),
297	zero private API. `main.ts`'s existing cursor-nudge dispatch (already public-API, unchanged)
298	still supplies the transaction that gives the comparison a chance to run.
299	
300	**Code cost**: ~35 lines added to `decorate.ts` (`computeGuides`, a second tree walk),
301	~140-line addition to `decorations.ts` (a hand-rolled, debounced `ViewPlugin` reusing
302	`@codemirror/view`'s own exported `RectangleMarker` as a positioned-div factory, not CM6's
303	higher-level `layer()` helper — see the in-code rationale for why), ~20-line `styles.css`.
304	
305	**Bugs found and fixed** (all three caught only by real e2e rendering/measurement — the
306	*pure* `computeGuides` unit tests were correct throughout; every bug was in the CM6
307	coordinate-system plumbing, exactly the kind of thing the postmortem warned unit tests
308	can't see):
309	
310	1. **Guides rendered ~76px too high, caught by an e2e rect assertion, root-caused via live
311	   DOM diagnostics.** `view.lineBlockAt(pos).top/.bottom` are relative to `view.documentTop`
312	   (itself viewport-relative, moves with scroll) — not to the guide layer's own container
313	   origin (`scrollDOM`'s top-left, where the container's `top: 0; left: 0` anchors it). The
314	   two differ by a real, non-zero, confirmed-live constant (`.cm-content`'s own offset within
315	   the scroller). Fixed by converting through `documentTop` the same way `coordsAtPos`'s
316	   result already gets converted through a `getBase()`-style subtraction for the x-axis —
317	   the asymmetry (only x was being converted, not y) is what let this slip through code
318	   review.
319	2. **A list item's guide anchored ~12px right of its own bullet, caught by an e2e rect
320	   assertion against `.cm-formatting-list`.** `coordsAtPos(pos, 1)` ("after"-biased) at a
321	   line's very first position returns the *far* edge of the first character — past the
322	   bullet glyph, not at its start. `coordsAtPos(pos, -1)` ("before"-biased) gives the
323	   character's own left edge, matching the bullet's true rendered start.
324	3. **A doubly-nested list item's guide anchored a full level too far left, caught by the
325	   same assertion at a deeper fixture level.** A nested list item's raw source line *starts
326	   with its own indentation whitespace* (`"  - nested item"`); Obsidian visually collapses
327	   that leading whitespace via its native indent mechanism rather than rendering it at
328	   normal character width, so `coordsAtPos` at the raw line's first position (a space)
329	   lands at the *parent* list level's column, not the marker's. Fixed by skipping past
330	   `text.length - text.trimStart().length` characters before measuring — general, not
331	   list-item-specific (a no-op for headings/paragraphs, which have no leading whitespace).
332	
333	All three were "invisible" in a full-page screenshot glance (subtle 1px, low-opacity lines)
334	and only surfaced by comparing exact measured coordinates — directly reinforcing the
335	postmortem's central lesson (DOM assertions test that code ran; here, pixel-level rect
336	assertions were what actually caught bugs a screenshot glance and a passing unit-test suite
337	both missed). A closer crop of one screenshot region did independently confirm the fix
338	visually once the coordinates were right.
339	
340	**Multi-line continuation (the case never even attempted last time)**: confirmed via e2e
341	that `lineBlockAt`'s block-level `.top`/`.bottom` correctly span a Shift+Enter-continued
342	node's full rendered height "for free," for both a paragraph and a list item — no special
343	handling needed once the coordinate-conversion bugs above were fixed.
344	
345	### Experiment 2b — guides via CSS stacked-gradient
346	
347	**Verdict: keep.** Branched off Experiment 1 (`experiment/decorations-2b-guides-css-gradient`).
348	All 8 corpus fixtures plus 4 real vault notes screenshotted, both themes, plus computed-style
349	assertions on the actual rendered `::before` pseudo-element (not just the raw custom property
350	we set) — see [e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts),
351	46/46 e2e tests green across the full suite. `computeLineGuides` (a new, single-pass function
352	in `decorate.ts`) gives every line the ascending tree depths of its strict, non-list-item
353	ancestors that "own" a guide (same list-item-ancestor exclusion as 2a, same real-vault
354	reasoning); `decorations.ts` turns that into one `--to-guides` custom property per line — a
355	comma-joined list of `repeating-linear-gradient(...)` layers, one per active depth — merged
356	into the same `Decoration.line` that already carries `--to-depth`, consumed by a single
357	`::before` (`@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers`'s technique, per the plan). Guides render
358	correctly through every kind — block, atom, list item, and (after one native-CSS override)
359	widget-replaced atoms, tables included after a later fix (see the "second round of real-vault
360	review" below) — confirmed live by a human using the actual table scrollbar in a real running
361	vault, no defects found.
362	
363	**A first pass concluded margin-shifted lines (atoms/list items) could never render a guide at
364	all — this was wrong, caught by the user pushing back rather than accepting the claim, and
365	corrected by actually testing it instead of reasoning from the box model alone.** Recorded here
366	in full because the correction process is as load-bearing as the result — this is exactly the
367	kind of unverified claim the original postmortem's whole point was to stop shipping:
368	
369	1. **The wrong claim**: a `::before` pseudo's background is clipped to its own box, and since
370	   `margin-left` (used for atoms/list items, Experiment 1's own fix for the padding-doesn't-
371	   move-the-box bug) shifts that box rightward, no `background-position` could reach a
372	   shallower ancestor's column — concluded "confirmed structural limitation" and shipped with
373	   e2e tests asserting the *absence* of a guide on those lines.
374	2. **The user's challenge**: after seeing the shipped result (guides missing on list/atom
375	   lines and gapping through real content), asked directly whether this was really a
376	   limitation of the technique or an implementation gap — refusing to accept the prior
377	   "confirmed" framing at face value.
378	3. **What was actually wrong**: a pseudo-element's own box does *not* have to match its
379	   containing block's dimensions — `left`/`right` can widen it arbitrarily, including
380	   leftward past where the line's own (shifted) box starts. Confirmed live: widening the
381	   pseudo by exactly `--to-own-shift` (the line's own margin-left, fully known — `depth * unit`
382	   for atoms, `supplementalDepth * unit` for list items, since Obsidian's native list hang uses
383	   `padding-left`/`text-indent`, neither of which move the box either) correctly renders the
384	   guide at the right global column, on both atom and list-item lines, confirmed by screenshot
385	   and by measuring `getBoundingClientRect()` on a list line (its box's own left edge exactly
386	   matched its `margin-left`, despite nonzero native `padding-left`/`text-indent` also being
387	   present — neither moves the box, only `margin` does).
388	4. **A second, real (narrower) obstacle, found only on widget-replaced atoms**: table/callout/
389	   hr/html still showed nothing, even with the same fix. Bisecting a probe `::before` at
390	   several `left` offsets (fully visible at `left: 0`, invisible at any negative offset)
391	   pointed at clipping, not a stacking/z-index issue. The actual cause: Obsidian's own
392	   `app.css` sets `contain: paint !important` on `.cm-content > [contenteditable="false"]`
393	   (all four widget kinds) — paint containment clips *all* descendant painting to the
394	   element's own box regardless of `overflow`, a mechanism the postmortem's own
395	   padding/margin cascade lesson doesn't cover. The exact same "matching beats escalating"
396	   fix applied: a 2-class override lost on specificity until it matched Obsidian's own
397	   3-selector chain, confirmed by checking which stylesheet rule actually won
398	   (`document.styleSheets` inspection, not guesswork).
399	5. **Table specifically needed one more fix, for a different and legitimate reason**: it
400	   *also* carries its own `overflow-x: auto` (for horizontal scroll on wide tables — a real
401	   feature, confirmed via computed style) on the same element the `contain` fix targets.
402	   Overriding `contain` alone doesn't surface a guide there; naively also forcing
403	   `overflow: visible` was first assumed unsafe, then confirmed unsafe by actually trying it
404	   (see the "second round of real-vault review" below for the full account) — but a working
405	   fix was then found (decoupling the guide's own box from the table's internal scroll
406	   container) and confirmed live by a human using the real scrollbar, no defects found.
407	
408	**Contrast with Experiment 2a**: 2a's overlay `<div>`s paint in a layer entirely outside
409	`.cm-content`, so a per-line box shift never mattered to it in the first place — this is a
410	real, structural difference in how the two techniques handle the "atom/list box is shifted"
411	case, it just isn't the *all-or-nothing* difference the first pass concluded. 2b needed a
412	couple of extra, narrow CSS overrides (matching Obsidian's own `contain: paint` selector, and
413	decoupling the table's scroll container — see below) that 2a never needed; 2a needed the full
414	pixel-measurement machinery that 2b never needed. Both techniques now handle every corpus
415	fixture correctly, tables included — 2a never had an equivalent gap to begin with, since its
416	overlay doesn't care what's inside `.cm-content` at all.
417	
418	**What works unconditionally, verified via the exact fixtures 2a used**: bridging through
419	every kind (block, atom, list item), and multi-line (Shift+Enter) continuation "for free" with
420	*no* special code — a structural side effect of this project's line model (every physical
421	source line is its own CM6 `.cm-line` with its own independent `Decoration.line`, not a single
422	logical block CM6 soft-wraps), so `computeLineGuides` just assigns the same `guideDepths` to a
423	node's continuation lines as its first line. This contrasts with 2a, which needed a dedicated
424	two-pass span computation (`computeGuides`) plus `lineBlockAt`'s block-level top/bottom
425	specifically to get multi-line coverage right.
426	
427	**No padding/margin-style cascade fight on the base mechanism**: `position: relative`/
428	`background` on `.cm-line.to-decor-guides` resolve exactly as set with no `!important` needed —
429	confirmed via `getComputedStyle`, both in e2e and by eye in the real vault. The *additional*
430	`contain: paint` fight on widget atoms (point 4 above) is a genuinely different mechanism this
431	initial verification pass didn't anticipate, caught only by the user's follow-up push and a
432	live bisection test, not by the original "confirm rather than assume" pass — a reminder that
433	"confirm" needs to mean actually trying the failing case, not just reasoning about why it
434	should fail.
435	
436	**Code cost**: ~55 lines added to `decorate.ts` (`computeLineGuides`, a single-pass tree walk —
437	no two-pass span bookkeeping needed, since a per-line fact only needs "which ancestors are
438	active *here*," not any node's full subtree extent), ~75-line addition to `decorations.ts` (a
439	pure function building the gradient string, folded into the existing `lineDecoration`/
440	`computeDecorations`, plus extending the existing `AtomWidgetMargins` ViewPlugin to also set
441	the same guide custom properties directly on widget DOM — no new `ViewPlugin`, no debounce, no
442	`StateEffect`/mode-toggle bookkeeping, since it rides the exact same `StateField` Experiment 1
443	already recomputes on every transaction), ~65-line `styles.css` (including the `contain`
444	override and its extensive justification comment). Meaningfully smaller and simpler than 2a's
445	~35+~140+~20 lines, and with a materially smaller surface for coordinate-system bugs — 2b has
446	none of the `coordsAtPos`/`lineBlockAt`/scroll-origin conversion code that produced all three
447	of 2a's real bugs, because it does no pixel measurement at all. Its own bugs were entirely in
448	a different category: cascade/specificity and CSS-containment fights, not coordinate math.
449	
450	**Bottom line for the head-to-head**: both techniques cover the corpus *completely* — every
451	kind, every gap line, tables included. Tables were, for a while, believed to be 2b's one
452	deliberate, structural gap (a trade against breaking real horizontal-scroll functionality) —
453	see finding #4 below for the full account of how that conclusion was revisited and a fix found
454	(decoupling the guide's own box from the table's internal scroll container via Obsidian's
455	existing `.table-wrapper` element), confirmed live by a human using the real scrollbar with no
456	defects found. 2b reaches full parity with 2a at the cost of two small, narrow, well-understood
457	CSS overrides (`contain: none`, and `.table-wrapper`'s `overflow-x: auto`) that 2a doesn't need.
458	2b is simpler, has zero pixel-measurement code, and rides the existing StateField with no new
459	ViewPlugin/debounce machinery. Given equivalent capability, 2b's smaller code size and simpler
460	mental model (no coordinate systems, no scroll calibration) make it the preferable technique
461	going forward — though the two are close enough that either would be a reasonable choice, and
462	2a's overlay approach remains proven and viable too.
463	
464	**Second round of real-vault review found four more real issues.** Every one was caught only
465	because the user pushed on a specific rendering detail rather than accepting a screenshot
466	glance or a DOM-level "looks correct" check — directly reinforcing the original postmortem's
467	central lesson a second time, on a technique that had already been through one round of
468	"confirm rather than assume" and still had these left:
469	
470	1. **Blockquotes: the native colored bar was being silently deleted, not just misaligned.**
471	   Reported as "the colored side-line stays at the leftmost position while the text indents
472	   correctly" — investigation found Obsidian implements a blockquote's own left bar via a
473	   native `::before` (`border-left`, confirmed via computed style with outline mode off). The
474	   guide rule also used `::before` on the same element — not a doubling, a full replacement (an
475	   element has exactly one `::before`), so activating a guide on a blockquote line silently
476	   deleted its native bar entirely; what the user saw was almost certainly the guide itself
477	   rendered where the (now-vanished) native bar used to be, not the native bar surviving.
478	   Confirmed `::after` is unused (`content: none`) by every kind this touches, including all
479	   four widget kinds, before switching the guide mechanism to it — now both the native bar and
480	   the guide render simultaneously, confirmed live. **Status: fixed, e2e-covered.**
481	
482	2. **Community themes (and, in principle, any wide-enough viewport) broke indentation
483	   entirely for margin-based lines — atoms and list items rendered to the LEFT of a same-level
484	   or shallower heading.** Root cause: Obsidian's own "readable line width" feature centers
485	   `.cm-line` content via a `margin-inline: auto`-driven rule applied to literally any `<div>`
486	   child of `.cm-content` (not theme-specific — the same rule exists in the bundled themes too,
487	   it just so happened to resolve near 0px at the viewport size used for earlier verification,
488	   masking it). This rule's *specified* value is the literal keyword `auto`, not a length —
489	   `--content-margin`'s own computed value is the string `"auto"` — so it can't be referenced
490	   in a `calc()` expression the way `contain`'s culprit rule could be matched by specificity.
491	   The margin-left rules for atoms/lists were *replacing* this native centering base instead of
492	   adding to it. Fixed by extending the existing widget-margin ViewPlugin (renamed
493	   `MarginCompensation`) to also patch plain `.cm-line` atoms/lists: read the native base live
494	   from an undecorated reference line (impossible from a StateField, which has no rendered DOM
495	   to measure — only a ViewPlugin, running after render, can), then combine
496	   `nativeBase + ownContribution` as one inline `!important` override. Verified the guide's own
497	   `--to-own-shift` calc needed NO changes: the native base is uniform across every line
498	   (decorated or not), so it cancels out of the *difference* between any two lines' columns,
499	   which is all the guide positioning ever needed. **Status: fixed, e2e-covered, confirmed live
500	   against the Minimal community theme by screenshot.**
501	
502	3. **The guide visibly broke at every blank line between sibling blocks, AND right after any
503	   heading/paragraph with children (before its own first child).** A screenshot-glance miss (a
504	   1-line gap reads as "close enough") but a real, confirmed regression against 2a, whose overlay
505	   is one continuous rectangle per guide and covers gaps between siblings for free. Root cause:
506	   `computeLineGuides` had no fact at all for blank `trailingGap` lines, so the per-line mechanism
507	   drew nothing there. Fixed in `computeLineGuides` in two stages: first, a LEAF node's own
508	   trailing gap (before the next sibling, at that leaf's own level) got a fact carrying the same
509	   `guideDepths` as its own content. That alone was shipped with the "before first child" case
510	   *deliberately* left uncovered, reasoning it matched Experiment 2a's own span (which also
511	   starts at the first child's own line) — but the user's continued real-vault review found this
512	   read as a real, visible break too, not acceptable parity, so it was covered in a second pass:
513	   a node WITH children's own trailing gap now uses `childGuideDepths` (the same depths its first
514	   child gets), since that gap is already "inside" the node's own subtree. This made
515	   `computeLineGuides`'s output a strict superset of `decorate()`'s line coverage instead of a 1:1
516	   zip target, so `decorations.ts` switched from index-zipping the two arrays to keying both by
517	   line number. **Status: fixed (fully, both cases), e2e-covered — a genuine improvement over 2a's
518	   own behavior in this respect, not mere parity with it.**
519	
520	4. **Tables: guide visibility vs. the table's own horizontal scroll, resolved through several
521	   rounds of "confirm rather than assume" that each corrected the previous round.** This finding
522	   evolved more than the other three and is recorded in full because the *process* is the
523	   reusable lesson:
524	   - **First pass** concluded this was a permanent, structural 2b-vs-2a difference: 2a's overlay
525	     lives entirely outside `.cm-content`, so a table's own internal horizontal scroll can't
526	     affect it (confirmed by literally scrolling a wide table under 2a and re-measuring its
527	     overlay's rect — byte-identical). 2b's guide is a pseudo-element *inside* the table widget's
528	     own box, so it's unavoidably subject to whatever clips or scrolls that box — table's own
529	     `overflow-x: auto` (for horizontal scroll of wide tables) clips the guide's leftward-widened
530	     `::after` exactly like `contain: paint` did, and overriding `overflow` to `visible` naively
531	     was assumed unsafe.
532	   - **User asked for the naive override to be tried live, not just reasoned about.** It was: a
533	     15-column, genuinely-unwrappable-content table (`scrollWidth` 3075px vs `clientWidth` 708px)
534	     with `overflow: visible !important` forced showed the predicted regression for real — table
535	     content spilled off the visible pane with no scrollbar, `scrollLeft` became inert (stuck at
536	     0, confirmed via script), and the WHOLE document became horizontally scrollable instead of
537	     just the table (nothing else up the ancestor chain clips it either). Confirmed unsafe, not
538	     just theorized.
539	   - **Investigating *why* it cascades (not just accepting the regression) found a real fix**:
540	     Obsidian's table widget conveniently already wraps the actual `<table>` (plus its add-row/
541	     add-column buttons) in an inner `.table-wrapper` div, distinct from the outer element the
542	     `contain`/`overflow` rules target. Moving `overflow-x: auto` onto `.table-wrapper` instead —
543	     while leaving the OUTER element `overflow: visible` — decouples the two concerns onto two
544	     different boxes: the outer no longer has anything to clip (confirmed: its own `scrollWidth`
545	     now equals its `clientWidth`, since its only child now scrolls internally instead of
546	     overflowing it), so the guide's leftward bleed is unobstructed, while `.table-wrapper`
547	     independently owns the actual wide-content scrolling.
548	   - **A real self-inflicted regression during write-up**: when transcribing this fix from a
549	     scratch/probe `<style>` tag into the actual `styles.css`, the outer element's own
550	     `overflow: visible !important` was dropped — reasoning (wrongly, without re-testing) that
551	     the new `.table-wrapper` rule would be sufficient alone. It is not: both are needed
552	     simultaneously (outer visible so the guide isn't clipped by *it*; wrapper auto so the real
553	     content still scrolls, contained). The probe had "worked" only because it was layered on
554	     top of a styles.css that *still* had the outer override from an earlier step — dropped when
555	     consolidating. Caught only because the user tried the rebuilt plugin in their own real
556	     Obsidian instance and reported the guide had disappeared, then asked for it to be
557	     double-checked rather than accepting a re-assurance — the exact same discipline that caught
558	     every other finding in this document, now catching a regression in the fix-verification
559	     process itself.
560	   - **Status: CONFIRMED.** Both rules are present and correct in `styles.css`, confirmed via
561	     computed style that both conditions hold simultaneously (outer `overflow-x: visible` and no
562	     longer overflowing itself; wrapper `overflow-x: auto` and still overflowing, i.e.
563	     scrollable; guide's `::after` background resolved and non-none) — AND confirmed by the user
564	     actually using the table's scrollbar (trackpad/click-drag) with the rebuilt plugin in their
565	     own real Obsidian instance: "it actually works... I don't see any notable defects or UX
566	     issues." This closes the last remaining gap in the whole guide feature: every kind, every
567	     gap line, and tables, all fully continuous, with 2b needing only two small, well-understood,
568	     narrowly-scoped CSS overrides (`contain: none`, and `.table-wrapper`'s `overflow-x: auto`)
569	     beyond the base mechanism — full parity with 2a's coverage, at meaningfully lower code cost
570	     and zero pixel measurement.
571	
572	All four were verified live (computed style, rect measurements, a bisection test, or a real
573	human trying the rebuilt plugin), not assumed from reasoning alone — the SAME discipline the
574	postmortem asked for, applied repeatedly, including to catch a mistake made while applying it.
575	Full e2e suite (unit + e2e) re-verified green after each fix; see
576	[e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts) for the
577	blockquote-coexistence, margin-compensation, gap-continuity, and table (computed-style side of
578	the fix) regression tests. The human-scrollbar-interaction side is, by nature, not something an
579	automated e2e assertion can fully substitute for — the computed-style test is a floor, not a
580	replacement for a periodic real-vault spot check if this area is touched again later.
581	
582	### Experiment 3 — minimal marker fallback
583	
584	Not triggered in practice. Its trigger condition (flat fixture unreadable) technically
585	fires, but the user's real-content read is that it's not actually a legibility gap (see
586	Experiment 1's "also confirmed not a bug" above). Markers are judged less important than
587	initially scoped now that indentation alone reads as hierarchy.
588	
589	### Experiment 4 — widget-spacer spike
590	
591	Not triggered. Experiment 1 showed no cascade fragility against the synthetic corpus or
592	real vault notes once the three bugs above were fixed — nothing suggests the
593	`padding-left`/`margin-left` approach is running out of road.
594	
595	### Open question: shrinking only our own added list margin
596	
597	Raised by the user, not yet decided. The deferred list-hang issue (above) is native
598	Obsidian chrome and explicitly out of scope for direct edits in this experiment. But a
599	narrower variant stays inside the additive-only discipline: **reduce only the margin *we*
600	add** to list items — not native `text-indent`/`padding-left` — by the list's own native
601	hang width, read live via `getComputedStyle` the same way the table fix (bug #3 above)
602	reads and compensates for native padding. Worth exploring as a follow-up, with two open
603	risks to resolve before trying it: (a) clamping so a shallow `supplementalDepth` never goes
604	negative once the hang is subtracted (the table fix's `max(0px, ...)` pattern applies
605	directly), and (b) the compensation must be based on the list **root**'s own hang, not each
606	item's — nested items can have wider markers (e.g. `10.` vs `-`) with different native hang
607	widths, and compensating per-item instead of per-root-chain would reintroduce exactly the
608	kind of within-list misalignment the wide-numbering fixture exists to catch.
609	
610	## Non-obvious findings
611	
612	Cross-experiment learnings, carried forward the same way the postmortem's own "carried-forward
613	technical findings" section was meant to be used — read this before starting 2a/2b/3/4:
614	
615	- **CM6 `Decoration.line` has zero effect on Obsidian's "embed-block" replacement widgets**
616	  (tables, callouts, raw HTML, and horizontal rules) — not a partial win, not a class-merge;
617	  confirmed live that both class and inline style come back completely empty. Any atom kind
618	  Obsidian renders this way needs direct DOM patching via a `ViewPlugin`'s `docViewUpdate`
619	  hook, not a CM6 decoration. Code fences and plain blockquotes are *not* in this category —
620	  they render as genuinely plain `.cm-line`s and decorate normally.
621	- **A single broad selector plus a fact lookup by document line number is enough** to handle
622	  all four widget-replaced kinds uniformly (`.cm-embed-block, .cm-line.hr` → `posAtDOM` →
623	  `decorate()` facts by line) — no need for kind-specific branches.
624	- **Matching beats escalating.** When Obsidian's own CSS wins a specificity fight even
625	  against `!important`, the fix is matching its ancestor-chain specificity, not adding more
626	  `!important` (there's nowhere further to escalate past equal importance — the tie-break is
627	  specificity, then source order).
628	- **An inline style with `!important`, set via JS, always wins over any stylesheet rule**
629	  regardless of that rule's own specificity — a reliable escape hatch specifically for
630	  native-widget cascade fights that a CSS-only rule can't win.
631	- **Padding's visibility depends on where the background lives.** Padding never moves an
632	  element's own box (carried forward from the original postmortem), but whether that's
633	  visually invisible or visually offsetting depends on whether the element's own background
634	  fills the padded box (invisible, as for callout) or the visible content is nested one level
635	  further in without its own covering background (offsetting, as for a `<table>` inside a
636	  padded wrapper).
637	- **Read native values live instead of hardcoding compensations.** Subtracting a
638	  `getComputedStyle(...)`-read native padding from an own-added margin (clamped at zero)
639	  stays correct across themes/Obsidian versions; a hardcoded pixel constant would silently
640	  drift the moment a theme changes that padding.
641	- **List items already sit right of sibling text in vanilla Obsidian** — the native
642	  `text-indent`/`padding-left` hang pair reserves room for the bullet regardless of outline
643	  mode. This experiment's additive design correctly preserved that native behavior
644	  (verified: our own added margin is identical across list/paragraph/blockquote at the same
645	  depth); the extra offset users notice is pre-existing Obsidian behavior becoming more
646	  visible now that everything else aligns precisely, not a regression this experiment
647	  introduced.
648	- **The synthetic corpus, even a deliberately adversarial one, missed all three real bugs
649	  above.** Every one was caught only by a human visually reviewing real, organic vault
650	  content (not synthetic fixtures, not DOM-attribute assertions). This reaffirms the
651	  postmortem's central lesson directly, in a new implementation: treat the real-vault pass
652	  as load-bearing verification for every future experiment, not a final formality after the
653	  fixtures pass.
654	- **CM6's coordinate systems are not interchangeable, and mixing them silently compiles.**
655	  `coordsAtPos()` is viewport-relative (like `getBoundingClientRect`); `lineBlockAt()`'s
656	  `top`/`bottom` are relative to `view.documentTop` instead — a *different* origin, even
657	  though both "sound" document-relative. Converting only one axis (x, via a `getBase()`-
658	  style subtraction) and not the other (y, left as the raw `lineBlockAt` value) type-checks
659	  fine and looks plausible in code review; it produced a ~76px vertical offset only caught
660	  by an e2e rect assertion against independently-measured `.cm-line` rects, never by the
661	  pure-function unit tests (which never touch real coordinates at all). Any experiment doing
662	  its own pixel measurement (2b's gradient positioning included, if it ends up needing any)
663	  should audit *every* axis's reference frame explicitly, not assume symmetry.
664	- **`coordsAtPos(pos, side)`'s side parameter matters at line starts, and defaults are not
665	  obviously safe.** `side: 1` ("after"-biased) at a line's very first position returns the
666	  far edge of the first character — for a list item, that's past the bullet glyph, not at
667	  its start. `side: -1` ("before"-biased) gives the character's own left edge. Untested,
668	  `side: 1` compiles and "looks right" (it did return *a* plausible-looking coordinate) —
669	  only a rect comparison against the actual rendered marker (`.cm-formatting-list`) caught
670	  the ~12px discrepancy.
671	- **A nested list item's raw source line starts with its own indentation whitespace, and
672	  Obsidian doesn't render that whitespace at literal character width.** Measuring
673	  `coordsAtPos` at a line's raw `.from` (before skipping leading whitespace) lands at
674	  wherever Obsidian's own indent mechanism collapses that whitespace to — which turned out
675	  to be the *parent* list level's own column, not the current line's marker. Any code
676	  measuring "where does this line's real content start" needs to skip
677	  `text.length - text.trimStart().length` characters first; this is general (headings/
678	  paragraphs have no leading whitespace, so it's a no-op there), not a list-item special case.
679	- **CM6 ships a first-party `layer()`/`RectangleMarker` pair (`@codemirror/view`, used
680	  internally for the selection/cursor layers) purpose-built for "measured overlay outside
681	  `.cm-content`, scrolls naturally."** `RectangleMarker` alone (just the positioned-div
682	  factory, not the full `layer()` wrapper) was reused here since `layer()`'s own `update()`
683	  hook only supports a synchronous redraw with no way to thread in the plan's requested
684	  debounce — but the full `layer()` extension is worth trying as-is for 2b or a future
685	  revisit if debouncing turns out not to matter in practice; it would remove the need for
686	  this experiment's manual scroll-coordinate calibration entirely.
687	- **A custom guide mechanism must actively coexist with Obsidian's own native "Show indent
688	  guides" setting for lists, not just avoid crashing next to it.** Real-vault side-by-side
689	  comparison (native only / both / ours only) showed native list guides are already precise
690	  (bullet to bullet) in a way a block-level, fixed-unit overlay can't match — running both
691	  at once visibly doubles lines; ours alone (if drawn for list nesting) reads as unevenly
692	  spaced. The fix wasn't CSS suppression of the native feature (obsidian-outliner's own
693	  approach, per the original postmortem) but scope reduction: never draw our guide where
694	  the *native* mechanism already owns the job (any list-item ancestor), only where native
695	  has zero representation at all (bridging a non-list ancestor into a list, or between
696	  non-list kinds). **2b will face this identical question** — a CSS-gradient guide is
697	  exactly as capable of doubling against native list guides as an overlay div is; the same
698	  list-item-ancestor exclusion should apply there too, not be treated as an 2a-specific fix.
699	- **A `::before` pseudo-element's own box does NOT have to match its containing block's
700	  dimensions — `left`/`right` can widen it arbitrarily, including leftward past where a
701	  `margin`-shifted line's own box starts.** A first pass on 2b wrongly concluded the opposite
702	  ("a per-line background can only ever paint within that line's own box, full stop") and
703	  shipped it as a "confirmed structural limitation" — this was corrected only after the user
704	  explicitly refused to accept that framing and asked for verification, not because the
705	  original box-model reasoning was re-examined unprompted. The actual fix: widen the pseudo by
706	  exactly the line's own known `margin-left` value (`--to-own-shift`), which brings its box's
707	  *left edge* back to the global column a shallower ancestor needs, and CSS raises no
708	  objection to a positioned descendant's box extending outside its containing block's own
709	  edges (only `overflow`/`contain` on an ancestor can clip that, and by default they don't).
710	  Confirmed live via a bisection test (a probe `::before` visible at `left: 0`, progressively
711	  clipped as `left` went more negative) that this is real, not a fluke. Generalizes beyond
712	  guide lines: any future per-line decoration that needs to reach a coordinate outside its own
713	  (possibly `margin`-shifted) line box can use the same widened-box technique instead of
714	  assuming it needs a measured overlay.
715	- **`contain: paint` (or `contain: strict`/`content`, which includes it) silently clips
716	  descendant painting to an element's own box regardless of `overflow` — a distinct mechanism
717	  from the `overflow` property, easy to miss when auditing "what could be clipping this."**
718	  Obsidian's own `app.css` sets it (`!important`, on a `.cm-content > [contenteditable="false"]`
719	  selector) on all four widget-replaced atom kinds (table/callout/hr/html), likely a rendering/
720	  virtualization isolation hint. It was found only by checking `getComputedStyle(...).contain`
721	  directly on the clipped element itself — `overflow` alone reported `visible` the whole time,
722	  which is what made the clipping look inexplicable at first. Overriding it needs the same
723	  "match Obsidian's own specificity, don't just add `!important`" fix Experiment 1 already
724	  established for `margin`/`padding` — a *different* property, same lesson, confirmed by
725	  checking which stylesheet rule actually won via `document.styleSheets` inspection rather than
726	  guessing from the rendered result.
727	- **Overriding a native CSS property should be checked for a legitimate reason before assuming
728	  it's safe to fight — but "it has a legitimate reason" isn't automatically "so leave it
729	  alone," it can also mean "so find where else that reason's actual mechanism lives."**
730	  Table's `overflow-x: auto` (for horizontal scroll on genuinely wide tables) sits on the same
731	  element a guide's `contain`/`overflow` overrides target, and naively forcing
732	  `overflow: visible` there does trade away that real, functional native behavior for a
733	  cosmetic guide line — confirmed live, not just theorized (a 15-column table lost its
734	  scrollbar entirely, `scrollLeft` became inert, and the *whole document* became horizontally
735	  scrollable instead of just the table). The productive next question wasn't "so don't do
736	  this," it was "why does this one property change break something else, and can that
737	  something else live on a different element?" It can: Obsidian's table widget already wraps
738	  the actual `<table>` in its own inner `.table-wrapper` div, distinct from the outer element
739	  carrying the `contain`/`overflow` fight. Moving `overflow-x: auto` onto that inner wrapper
740	  instead of the outer decouples "let our pseudo-element bleed past this box's edge" from "clip
741	  the actual wide content that needs to scroll" — two different concerns that don't have to
742	  live on the same box just because they happened to start out on the same native element.
743	  Generalizes: when overriding a native property seems to force a real trade-off, check whether
744	  the *conflicting* responsibility can be relocated to a different element in the existing DOM
745	  before accepting the trade-off as permanent.
746	- **A script-driven interaction (setting `.scrollLeft` programmatically, reading it back) is
747	  real evidence that a scroll container is *functional*, but it is not equivalent to a human
748	  actually using it.** It doesn't exercise trackpad/wheel gesture handling, doesn't reveal
749	  whether a scrollbar is visually discoverable, and doesn't catch problems that only show up
750	  under real input events. A fix that passes every computed-style and scripted-interaction
751	  check should still be handed to a human for a final pass before being called settled if the
752	  thing being fixed is itself a human-facing interaction (as opposed to, say, a purely visual
753	  property like a color or an alignment, where a computed-style check IS the full story).
754	- **Consolidating a fix from a scratch/probe test into the "real" file is itself a step that
755	  needs re-verification, not just a mechanical copy.** A probe's injected `<style>` tag can
756	  "work" only because it's layered on top of OTHER state (here, a still-present earlier
757	  override in `styles.css`) that gets dropped when the probe's finding is folded into a single,
758	  final rule — silently losing a piece that was necessary but easy to assume the new rule
759	  subsumed. Caught only because a human tried the rebuilt output and reported a regression,
760	  not because the "surely this is equivalent" reasoning was double-checked before shipping it.
761	
762	## Setup (done, 2026-07-13)
763	
764	- Full attempt-3 snapshot (broken code + the docs as they stood) preserved on
765	  `archive/outline-decorations-attempt-3` — reference only, do not build on it.
766	- `feat/outline-decorations` reset to a clean baseline: `decorate.ts` + its unit tests kept
767	  (never the buggy layer), `decorations.ts`/`styles.css`/the old e2e spec dropped (rebuild
768	  per experiment, not patched in place), `main.ts` reverted to no decoration wiring,
769	  `design.md`/`spec.md`/`tasks.md` annotated paused-with-context rather than left describing
770	  a scope no code matches, package.json/.gitignore's `styles.css`-symlink fix kept, shared
771	  fixture corpus committed at `e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts`.
772	- `experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent` branched off the clean baseline, ready to start.
773	- `experiment/decorations-2a-*`/`2b-*` branch off **1**, not off the clean baseline, once it
774	  validates — guides need some indentation already rendered to meaningfully measure/draw
775	  against. Create with `git checkout experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent && git
776	  checkout -b experiment/decorations-2a-guides-overlay` (and `-2b-guides-css-gradient`
777	  similarly) once 1 is done.
778	- Experiments 3 and 4 are conditional/optional — branch only if triggered (see below).
779	
780	## Handoff prompts
781	
782	Self-contained prompts, one per experiment — paste into a fresh session (or hand to a
783	subagent) to start that experiment in isolation. Each assumes zero prior context beyond
784	what's inline plus the two linked docs.
785	
786	### Experiment 1 — additive-only indentation, no marker
787	
788	> Branch: `experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent` (already created off a clean
789	> `feat/outline-decorations`, no decoration code present — `decorate.ts` and its tests are
790	> the only survivors from a prior failed attempt, kept because they were never the buggy
791	> layer). Read `docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md` and
792	> `docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md` (Experiment 1's section) for full
793	> context before starting.
794	>
795	> Build: paragraphs/headings/atoms get plain `padding-left`/`margin-left` = `depth × unit`
796	> (a fixed rem/px constant, never `em` — the postmortem's marker-size bug was exactly an
797	> `em` resolving against the wrong element's font-size; don't repeat that class of bug for
798	> *any* property here). List items must **never** have `text-indent`/`padding-left`
799	> touched — instead add a NEW computed quantity, supplemental depth = total tree depth −
800	> depth-within-nearest-list-root (i.e. how many non-list-item ancestors sit above the
801	> nearest list root), and apply only `margin-left` = `supplemental-depth × unit` on top of
802	> native rendering. No synthetic marker glyph on any kind — list items keep their fully
803	> native, untouched bullet/number.
804	>
805	> `decorate.ts` already computes total depth; add the second counter (resets to 0 whenever
806	> a list-item ancestor chain starts) as a new field on `LineDecorationFact`, extending the
807	> existing unit tests rather than replacing them. `decorations.ts`/`styles.css` get rebuilt
808	> from scratch (nothing to salvage from the archived attempt) using this additive-only
809	> discipline.
810	>
811	> Test against every fixture in `e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts` (not just the one that seems
812	> relevant) — screenshot each in both bundled light and dark themes. Specifically verify:
813	> the heading-then-list fixture shows the list shifted right by the heading's depth
814	> contribution while its own internal per-level spacing stays pixel-identical to
815	> outline-mode-off; the wide-numbering fixture shows no marker/text overlap; multi-line
816	> continuation fixtures indent their continuation lines identically to the node's first
817	> line. Explicitly check the fold-indicator (collapse chevron on list items with children)
818	> for collisions — the one native element that already burned an attempt this cycle.
819	>
820	> `npm run build` only runs `tsc --noEmit` — it does NOT rebuild `main.js`. Use `npm run
821	> build:plugin` (or `npm run vault:install`) before any real-vault check, or you'll verify
822	> a stale bundle. A real dev-vault pass (not just the synthetic fixtures) is required before
823	> calling this done. Record the result in the comparison table in
824	> `docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md`.
825	
826	### Experiment 2a — guide lines via pixel-measured overlay
827	
828	> Prerequisite: Experiment 1 validated and merged/available. Branch off it:
829	> `git checkout experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent && git checkout -b
830	> experiment/decorations-2a-guides-overlay`. Read
831	> `docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md` and
832	> `docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md` (Experiment 2 section) first.
833	>
834	> Build a `ViewPlugin` that, after each debounced recompute (gate on `docChanged ||
835	> viewportChanged`, NOT unconditionally on every `ViewUpdate` — the prior attempt's
836	> per-ViewUpdate recompute was more expensive than needed), reads already-rendered pixel
837	> positions via `view.coordsAtPos()`/`view.lineBlockAt()` and draws one absolutely-
838	> positioned overlay `<div>` per ancestor level, inline-styled (`el.style.top/left/height`
839	> set directly from JS, not via CSS classes) into a layer living outside `.cm-content` —
840	> this is `obsidian-outliner`'s proven technique (`VerticalLines.ts` in its source), not
841	> something to reinvent from scratch; read that file if you want the exact reference
842	> shape. Handle the out-of-band mode-toggle refresh with a dedicated `StateEffect` rather
843	> than an empty `view.dispatch({})` forcing a full recompute path.
844	>
845	> Test against every fixture in `e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts`, in both themes. The specific
846	> case to get right that was never even attempted last time: the multi-line/wrapped
847	> continuation fixture — a guide line must span the full rendered height of a multi-line
848	> node (should come free from `lineBlockAt`, which is block-level and covers all wrapped
849	> visual rows — verify this rather than assuming it). `npm run build:plugin`/`vault:install`
850	> before any real-vault check. Record results in the comparison table.
851	
852	### Experiment 2b — guide lines via CSS stacked-gradient
853	
854	> Prerequisite: Experiment 1 validated and merged/available. Branch off it:
855	> `git checkout experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent && git checkout -b
856	> experiment/decorations-2b-guides-css-gradient`. Read the same two docs as 2a first — this
857	> is a deliberate head-to-head alternative to 2a, not a fallback; build both, don't assume
858	> a winner in advance.
859	>
860	> Build guide lines as a single `Decoration.line` per line, with one CSS custom property
861	> set to a comma-joined list of `repeating-linear-gradient(...)` layers (one per ancestor
862	> level), consumed by a single `::before` — this is the technique
863	> `@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers` uses (its `src/index.ts` is the reference if you
864	> want the exact shape): O(1) DOM nodes per line regardless of depth, no JS pixel
865	> measurement, no overlay layer. Because this reintroduces a real CSS rule participating in
866	> the normal cascade (unlike 2a's inline-styled overlay), explicitly verify it does NOT
867	> resurrect any of the specificity/`!important` problems from the original postmortem — it
868	> shouldn't, since guide-drawing isn't a property any native rule already contests, but
869	> confirm rather than assume.
870	>
871	> Same fixture corpus, same both-themes requirement, same multi-line-continuation focus as
872	> 2a (get the line's own rendered full height for the gradient — should be free since it's
873	> one element per whole line, but verify). Record results in the comparison table,
874	> specifically comparing code size/complexity and robustness against 2a.
875	
876	### Experiment 3 — minimal marker fallback (conditional)
877	
878	> Only start this if Experiment 1's real-vault/screenshot review of the **flat** fixture
879	> (`FLAT_MD` in `e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts`) shows indentation alone doesn't read as
880	> "outline mode is on" — everything's at depth 0, so indentation conveys nothing, which is
881	> the *original* motivating bug this whole change exists to fix
882	> (`docs/research/04-open-questions.md`: "with no bullets/indent chrome, outline mode is
883	> hard to verify by eye in flat documents"). Branch off whichever of 1/2a/2b is current
884	> baseline at the time.
885	>
886	> Build a marker that cannot inherit font-size by construction — e.g. a fixed-px-width left
887	> border or background tint on paragraph/heading/atom lines, not a `::before` glyph sized in
888	> `em`. Verify the flat fixture reads as distinct nodes at a glance in a screenshot. If this
889	> still isn't legible enough, the fallback-of-the-fallback is the overlay-measured marker
890	> (same mechanism as 2a, fixed absolute size) — more implementation work, held in reserve.
891	
892	### Experiment 4 — widget-spacer risk-spike (optional)
893	
894	> Only start this if Experiment 1's `padding-left`/`margin-left` approach keeps showing
895	> cascade fragility against real themes/content despite the additive-only discipline. Spike
896	> `Decoration.widget` (an inline spacer with real computed pixel width, pushing content via
897	> layout rather than any CSS box-model property) in isolation, specifically against the
898	> multi-line/wrapped-continuation fixture. Go in expecting it might fail: CM6 wraps every
899	> widget in an invisible `.cm-widgetBuffer` (a confirmed, maintainer-acknowledged,
900	> unresolved gotcha) that can introduce stray line-wrap points — exactly the risk for
901	> wrapped text. The whole CM6 community converged on CSS `Decoration.line` instead for this
902	> exact reason; this spike exists to find out definitively whether that gotcha actually
903	> manifests for us; do not treat this as a preferred direction going in.
904
